request.question: Elton John's songwriting partner for the song "Indian Sunset" was born in what year?

title: Come Down in Time
Elton John (album)
Ghetto Gospel
Ray Cooper
Elton John: Me, Myself & I
The Goaldiggers Song
Bernie Taupin
Elton John
Indian Sunset
I Don't Wanna Go on with You Like That
sentences: "Come Down in Time" is the second track on Elton John's third album, "Tumbleweed Connection", released in 1970.
The lyric was written by Bernie Taupin, Elton's long time writing partner.
The song was originally recorded for John's second album, "Elton John".
The song was played alongside the Sydney Orchestra in a 1986 live performance.
Elton John is the second studio album by English singer-songwriter Elton John, released on 10 April 1970 by DJM Records.
It was released by Uni Records as John's debut album in the United States; many people there assumed it was his first album, as "Empty Sky" was not released in that country until 1975.
"Elton John" includes his breakthrough single "Your Song", and helped to establish his career during what was considered the "singer-songwriter" era of popular music.
In the US, it was certified gold in February 1971 by the Recording Industry Association of America (RIAA).
In the same year, it was nominated for the Grammy Award for Album of the Year.
"Ghetto Gospel" is a song by rapper Tupac Shakur, which was originally released as the second single on his posthumous 2004 album "Loyal to the Game".
It samples Elton John's 1971 track, "Indian Sunset".
The single topped the charts in the United Kingdom (for 3 weeks), Australia, Czech Republic and the Republic of Ireland.
Raymond Thomas "Ray" Cooper (born 19 September 1947) is an English virtuoso percussionist.

He is a session and road-tour percussionist, and occasional actor, who has worked with several musically diverse bands and artists including George Harrison, Billy Joel, Rick Wakeman, Eric Clapton, Pink Floyd and Elton John.
Cooper absorbed the influence of rock drummers from the 1960s and 1970s such as Ginger Baker, Carmine Appice, and John Bonham.
Incorporation of unusual instruments (for rock drummers of the time) such as cowbells, glockenspiel, and tubular bells, along with several standard kit elements, helped create a highly varied setup.

Continually modified to this day, Cooper's percussion set offers an enormous array of percussion instruments for sonic diversity such as the tambourine, congas, crash cymbals, cowbells, roto toms, tubular bells, the gong, snare and timpani.
For two decades Cooper honed his technique; In the 1990s, he reinvented his style.

He is known for the 7-minute percussion and drum solos he performed during the years 1990–1991 for Eric Clapton and for the 7-minute percussion and drum solos during all the 1994 Face to Face Tours with Billy Joel, and Elton John, and the tours with the Elton John band during the years of 1994–1995.
Elton John: Me, Myself & I is a 2007 documentary filmed after the death of Elton John's good friend Diana and other soul shaking events that caused him to reassess his life.
It is a candid appraisal by Elton John (tongue in cheek) of his fame, drug use, sexuality, and mistakenly taking his life for granted.
It was filmed in Las Vegas, Nevada, United States
"The Goaldiggers Song" is a charity single by Elton John, released in 1977.
The song is written for the Goaldigger's charity to provide playing fields in under-privileged areas.
At the time in 1977, Elton John was living reclused in London; the only time he would go out was to go see his football team Watford F.C. play.
The single never charted anywhere, as it was a limited charity release-only 500 copies were pressed, approximately half of which were signed by Elton.
It was available for a cost of five UK pounds by mail-order only and featured Elton and a number of other British celebrities including Jimmy Hill and Eric Morecombe in conversation on the B-side.
The tapes were destroyed after the pressing, so no other official copies of the recording exist.

Bernard John Taupin (born 22 May 1950) is an English lyricist, poet, and singer, best known for his long-term collaboration with Elton John, writing the lyrics for the majority of the star's songs, and making his lyrics some of the best known in music history.
Sir Elton Hercules John, (born Reginald Kenneth Dwight; 25 March 1947), is an English singer, pianist, and composer.
He has worked with lyricist Bernie Taupin as his songwriting partner since 1967; they have collaborated on more than 30 albums to date.
In his five-decade career Elton John has sold more than 300million records, making him one of the best-selling music artists in the world.
He has more than fifty Top 40 hits, including seven consecutive No. 1 US albums, 58 "Billboard" Top 40 singles, 27 Top 10, four No. 2 and nine No. 1.
For 31 consecutive years (1970–2000) he had at least one song in the "Billboard" Hot 100.

His tribute single, re-penned in dedication to the late Princess Diana, "Candle in the Wind 1997" sold over 33million copies worldwide and is the best-selling single in the history of the UK and US singles charts.
He has also composed music, produced records, and has occasionally acted in films.
John owned Watford Football Club from 1976 to 1987, and 1997 to 2002.
He is an honorary Life President of the club, and in 2014 had a stand named after him at the club's home stadium.
"Indian Sunset" is a 1971 song, written and recorded by Elton John, with lyrics by his long-time songwriting partner, Bernie Taupin.
It was from John's album "Madman Across the Water".
The song chronicles the story of an unnamed American Indian warrior on the verge of defeat from the white man.
Taupin was inspired to write the lyrics after visiting a Native American reservation.
"I Don't Wanna Go on with You Like That" is a hit song from British performer Elton John's 1988 album, "Reg Strikes Back".
The song was written by John and his long-time songwriting partner, Bernie Taupin.
The song reached #30 on the UK Singles Chart.

It fared better in the United States, peaking at #2 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 chart in August 1988, becoming John's biggest hit of the 1980s there (kept out of the top spot by fellow British singer and vocal collaborator George Michael's hit single "Monkey").
The song also became John's eighth #1 on the adult contemporary chart.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

response.hop1_query: Elton John's songwriting partner for the song "Indian Sunset" was born in what year?
hop1_docs: Indian Sunset | "Indian Sunset" is a 1971 song, written and recorded by Elton John, with lyrics by his long-time songwriting partner, Bernie Taupin.  It was from John's album "Madman Across the Water".  The song chronicles the story of an unnamed American Indian warrior on the verge of defeat from the white man.  Taupin was inspired to write the lyrics after visiting a Native American reservation.

Elton John (album) | Elton John is the second studio album by English singer-songwriter Elton John, released on 10 April 1970 by DJM Records.  It was released by Uni Records as John's debut album in the United States; many people there assumed it was his first album, as "Empty Sky" was not released in that country until 1975.  "Elton John" includes his breakthrough single "Your Song", and helped to establish his career during what was considered the "singer-songwriter" era of popular music.  In the US, it was certified gold in February 1971 by the Recording Industry Association of America (RIAA).  In the same year, it was nominated for the Grammy Award for Album of the Year.

Come Down in Time | "Come Down in Time" is the second track on Elton John's third album, "Tumbleweed Connection", released in 1970.  The lyric was written by Bernie Taupin, Elton's long time writing partner.  The song was originally recorded for John's second album, "Elton John".  The song was played alongside the Sydney Orchestra in a 1986 live performance.

Ghetto Gospel | "Ghetto Gospel" is a song by rapper Tupac Shakur, which was originally released as the second single on his posthumous 2004 album "Loyal to the Game".  It samples Elton John's 1971 track, "Indian Sunset".  The single topped the charts in the United Kingdom (for 3 weeks), Australia, Czech Republic and the Republic of Ireland.

Love Songs (Elton John album) | Love Songs is a compilation album by British singer-songwriter Elton John.  The album was first released on 6 November 1995 by John's own label The Rocket Record Company, in conjunction with Mercury Records.  The album was released in North America by MCA Records almost a year after the European release, on 24 September 1996.  In the US, it was certified gold in December 1996, platinum in March 1997, 2× platinum in December 1998 and 3× platinum in August 2000 by the RIAA.

Elton John's Christmas Party | Elton John's Christmas Party is a Christmas-themed compilation album from British singer-songwriter Elton John.  It features his 1973 Christmas single, "Step into Christmas", and a new duet with Joss Stone, "Calling It Christmas".  According to John's introduction in the liner notes, as opposed to doing an album of his versions of already famous songs, he chose to do a compilation with some of his favorite holiday songs by other artists.

I Don't Wanna Go on with You Like That | "I Don't Wanna Go on with You Like That" is a hit song from British performer Elton John's 1988 album, "Reg Strikes Back".  The song was written by John and his long-time songwriting partner, Bernie Taupin.  The song reached #30 on the UK Singles Chart.  It fared better in the United States, peaking at #2 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 chart in August 1988, becoming John's biggest hit of the 1980s there (kept out of the top spot by fellow British singer and vocal collaborator George Michael's hit single "Monkey").  The song also became John's eighth #1 on the adult contemporary chart.
hop1_titles: I Don't Wanna Go on with You Like That
response.summary_1:
- [1] "'lyrics by his long-time songwriting partner, Bernie Taupin.'" (identifies Bernie Taupin as the song's lyricist)
- [1] "'Indian Sunset' is a 1971 song." (song date/context)
- [3] "'lyric was written by Bernie Taupin, Elton's long time writing partner.'" (corroborates Taupin as long-time partner)
- [7] "'written by John and his long-time songwriting partner, Bernie Taupin.'" (additional corroboration)
- Bridge entity: Bernie Taupin — the songwriting partner whose birth year is required.
- Missing/ambiguous fact: Bernie Taupin's birth year is not present in any supplied passage.
- Next-hop retrievals (prioritized):
  1) Fetch the Bernie Taupin page (e.g., en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bernie_Taupin) — extract the "Born" / birth_date from the infobox or the opening paragraph (report the birth year).
  2) If Wikipedia page unavailable or unclear, fetch authoritative biography sources (e.g., AllMusic biography, official site, or reliable news obituaries/profiles) — extract stated birth year.
  3) (If needed) Fetch a Bernie Taupin disambiguation page only if multiple persons named Bernie/Bernard Taupin appear, and then fetch the primary biography page for the songwriter to confirm the birth year.

No final answer provided because the birth year is not present in the supplied passages.
hop2_docs: List of songs with lyrics by Bernie Taupin | Bernie Taupin is a lyricist, perhaps most notable for writing many of the lyrics to the music of Elton John.  Over the years, he has written songs for a variety of other artists including Alice Cooper, Heart, Melissa Manchester, Starship, Rod Stewart and Richie Sambora.
Two Rooms: Celebrating the Songs of Elton John & Bernie Taupin | Two Rooms: Celebrating the Songs of Elton John & Bernie Taupin

Tribe (Bernie Taupin album) | Tribe is a 1987 solo album by Bernie Taupin issued by RCA Records.  The album includes the two singles, "Friend of the Flag" and "Citizen Jane".  Music videos were made for both singles.

Maxine Feibelman | Maxine Phyllis Feibelman (born 1952) is a former seamstress for the Elton John band who was married for several years to John's frequent collaborator Bernie Taupin.  Her romance, marriage, and break-up with Taupin influenced at least eight of Taupin's and John's songs, she provided the title and refrain for one of their songs, and she supported John's path toward his distinct stage costume style.

Paca Thomas | Paca Thomas is the owner of pacaworks.com, a multi-media production company.  He can also be heard weekly as Bernie Taupin's sidekick/producer on Bernie Taupin's American Roots Radio on SiriusXM's The Loft.  He is the son of Philadelphia DJ George Thomas.

Taupin | Taupin is lyricist Bernie Taupin's first solo album.  It is a spoken word album of his poetry.  Taupin is well known for his collaboration with Elton John writing the lyrics to the vast majority of songs on his albums, and he worked with the same musicians John used on his albums in order to create his own.  Gus Dudgeon produced the album and Steve Brown coordinated it.

Candle in the Wind | "Candle in the Wind" is a threnody with music and lyrics by Elton John and Bernie Taupin.  It was originally written in 1973, in honour of Marilyn Monroe, who had died 11 years earlier.
hop2_titles: List of songs with lyrics by Bernie Taupin
Two Rooms: Celebrating the Songs of Elton John & Bernie Taupin
response.summary_2: Bernie Taupin is Elton John's long-time songwriting partner and the lyricist for the 1971 song "Indian Sunset." Bernie Taupin is a lyricist noted for writing many of the lyrics to Elton John's music and released the 1987 solo album "Tribe."
